assignment_description:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Did you know that sentences do not occur in nature? Language comes from us; it is ourselves, in words!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.5-8

➜ DISCUSS
How is a word different from a random group of letters, like "dixerp?"

Parts of Speech: Noun and Verb
Parts of Sentence: Subject and Predicate
Phrases: No Subject Predicate allowed
Clauses: Subject Predicate required
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
The key to explaining the four levels of grammar revolves around the noun and the verb.

assignment_description:
What Is Grammar?
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.9-11

➜ DISCUSS
Think about a team sport. How are the players different from the game? How could this idea be applied to language?

The Parts of Speech
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
What do we mean when we say the "parts of a bird"? [the small bits that make up the whole bird] Our language has parts that make up the whole, too.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.13-19

➜ DIAGRAM
Copy the diagram on p.17. Color the parts with crayons or colored pencils to match the drawing. Which parts seem most important?

Nouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ RECAP
What are the two main kinds of words? [nouns and verbs]

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.20-21

➜ PLAY
What would it be like if you could only talk in nouns? Try it! Can it work? [Remember, you can only use words that NAME things!]

Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
How do we know when to use the word "I" and when to use "me"? Think of a sentence with each.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.22-24

➜ DISCUSS
The prefix "pro" means "for." A "Pro-noun" is a word you use "for a noun." "Ante" means "before," and "cede" means "to go." So, the noun that "went before" the pronoun is called its "antecedent."
Read the left column on p.24 aloud and time it; read the right column aloud and time it. Pronouns are quick!

Subject Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.25-27

➜ WRITE
Copy the lists of subject pronouns and object pronouns. Look at the last narration you wrote. Did you use any pronouns? Circle them with an aqua pencil.

Nouns & Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Two of the subject pronouns are "he" and "she." How do we know when to use which?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.28-29

➜ DISCUSS
"She" is a feminine pronoun, so it needs to replace a feminine noun.

Adjectives
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Quick! List as many kinds of tables as you can think of in one minute! Can you imagine if there was a whole separate noun for each one of these?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.30-33

➜ DISCUSS
Good writers use adjectives when they are needed. Too many adjectives make sentences too wordy and spoil the story.
Adjectives do not "describe" nouns from a distance but actually change them. The old car is a different car from the sports car. Adjectives modify.

Adjectives (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ PRACTICE
Complete the activity on p.34 of Grammar Island. Why are some adjectives funny and others not funny?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.35-36

➜ PRACTICE
Draw a purple box around each of the seven adjectives that modify the word "duck."

Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the instructions. Use a purple box for the adjective and an aqua circle for the pronouns.
Grammar Island p.37-38

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.39-41

Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.42

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the instructions. Note that, for now, not every word will get a label. Check your work using the answer key and review any difficult concepts.
Grammar Island p.43

Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the instructions. Note that not all words will get a label. Check your work using the answer key and review any difficult concepts.
Grammar Island p.44

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.45

Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ PRACTICE
Copy and color the noun system graphic on p.45. Explain why it is structured the way it is.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.46-47

The Sentence
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.48-49

➜ DIAGRAM
Copy the diagram on p.48 using colored pencils. Notice how simple it is!

➜ PRACTICE
Write your own short sentence like the one on p.49. Separate the two sides with a line.

Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.50-52

➜ DISCUSS
On p.52, there are two sentences about ducks. One shows what the duck DOES. The other shows what the duck EQUALS. Can you figure out which is which?

Action Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.53-55

➜ PRACTICE
For each subject, provide an action verb that makes sense:
1\. The kangaroo
2\. Mom
3\. A lion
4\. Our car

Linking Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.56-60

➜ DISCUSS
In the sentence, "Sam has a hamster," why is "has" an action verb and not a linking verb?

Linking Verbs (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.61-62

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the instructions to identify the action and linking verbs. Try replacing the verb with "=" to test.
Grammar Island p.63. Check your answers on p.64.

Adverbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ RECAP
Some words, like "smells," can be either an action verb or a linking verb. How can that be? [ex. He smells a steak. / He smells good.]

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.65-69

➜ PRACTICE
Label the noun, verb, adjective, and adverb in the sentence on p.69.

Adverbs (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.70-71

➜ PRACTICE
Label each word in the sentences as pron., v., adj., n., or adv. Check your work using the Grammar Island Teacher Manual (See Quick Links).
Grammar Island p.72-73

Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
What is a "junction"? [a place where things come together, e.g., roads, railroads, etc.] Today's lesson is about a type of word that brings other words together.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.75-80

➜ DISCUSS
What would our language sound like if we did not have conjunctions? Try it with the sentences on p.79.
On p.80, why does the third pair make a good conjunction when the first two do not?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
For more practice with Conjunctions, see the PDF.
∞ PDF Link: Grammar Island Additional Practice

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar: Grade 4 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Grammar Island: Five Kinds of Words: Can you tell the part of speech of every word in these sentences? Put n. below each noun, pron. below each pronoun, adj. below each adjective, v. below each verb, and adv. below each adverb.

She is nice.

Polly wants a cracker badly.

The eagle chick was fuzzy.

A high cloud suddenly dropped blue rain.

assignment_description:
Prepositions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.81-84

➜ PRACTICE
Place a pencil somewhere in the room. Say three sentences to describe where the pencil is in space. What are the prepositions you used? Could you do it without using prepositions?

Prepositional Phrases
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Read the sentences on p.82 again. Notice the rhythm of the words in each prepositional phrase. Try clapping the rhythm.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.85-87

➜ RECITE
Do you remember the object pronouns? Recite as many as you can. If you need help, look back on p.26.

Interjections
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.88-89

➜ DISCUSS
Interjections are unique; they do not interact with other words. They do not modify, replace, or join. Why would "yes" and "no" be interjections?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
If interested, let students write their own story or do it together. Pick a part of speech, and then write a story that illustrates the nature of that part.

assignment_description:
Putting the Parts Together
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
•Go over each sentence on p.90-91 slowly and carefully, noticing how each word is labeled.
•Read p.92-93

➜ PRACTICE
Since Bertie could only hear modifiers, she only heard parts of what was said to her. Go back and figure out what she heard.

For groups or classrooms,

➜ PRACTICE
Label the parts of speech and parts of the sentence on p.114. Check your work.

➜ DISCUSS
We can now do two-level analysis. What is the difference between level one and level two?

Linking Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.115-119

➜ DISCUSS
• Remember to use the "=" test to identify linking verbs and subject complements.
• Why do we understand the subject more completely when there is a subject complement?

Action & Linking Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.120-124
Note that a subject complement can be a noun, a SUBJECT pronoun, or even an adjective. I am Joan; I am she; I am blue.

Clauses
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.137-141

➜ PRACTICE
Write a sentence that has two clauses.

➜ NOTE
A key concept is that the phrases are INSIDE the clauses. The clause includes the phrase. The phrase is a part of its clause.

⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ PRACTICE
Write a compound sentence. Remember the comma!

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Grammar: Grade 4 — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Practice Island: Is the word in brackets a noun, pronoun, adjective, verb, adverb, preposition, conjunction, or interjection?
_______ 1. Many fish circled in the blue water [under] the dock.
_______ 2. A [flock]of birds passed high over the island, flying south.
_______ 3. [The] sunset spread red and purple light over the sky.
_______ 4. [Yes], the heavy seas pounded the rocks on the north shore.
_______ 5. A frog sang [happily] in the grass at the edge of the sand.
Is the word in brackets a subject, predicate, direct object, or subject complement?
_______ 6. Grammar Island is a small [place], rocky and covered with pines.
_______ 7. A [school] of shiny silver fish jumped from the aqua waves.
_______ 8. The squirrel [chattered] noisily from the high branch.
_______ 9. An owl hooted in the moonlight, and we saw his [shadow].
_______ 10. It was a peaceful [night], and the water lapped gently at the shore.
Is the part in brackets a phrase or a clause?
_______ 11. The big waves rolled [over the rocks], exploding into foam.
_______ 12. [Grammar Island is a good home for many small animals].
_______ 13. [The ducks flew south this morning], and we watched them take off.
_______ 14. [Birds chirped].
_______ 15. The sandy beach was a good place to run [in the morning sun].
